# B21: Key Quotes & Strategic Questions

## Key Quotes

**"We actually see it a ton because people are just building these industries that there's been no technical progress in, but there's also no APIs."**
— Alex Forman
*Context:* Describing the unexpected ICP discovery — non-technical builders in underserved industries (garbage collection, etc.) who now can build software but lack developer infrastructure. Reveals a massive greenfield market for Parse.

**"How are we supposed to solve certain problems for society if we don't have inclusiveness for the sake of representing unsolved problems completely."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Connecting the lack of developer ecosystems in unsexy industries to a broader systemic problem — if the people closest to these problems can't build, the problems stay unsolved. Frames the Parse + Zo opportunity as more than commercial.

**"I only just developed a mental model for like, oh, you could have data regularly flow from one place into a centralizing pool that you can then do all sorts of cool shit with."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Describing his journey as a non-technical founder discovering API-driven data flows through Zo. Perfectly articulates the "aha moment" that Parse can accelerate for the non-technical ICP.

**"You don't really need to think about, oh, like, what's our rule set for this? Because you have LLMs that can actually just do this reasoning."**
— Alex Forman
*Context:* Recognizing the Zo + Parse synergy — Parse provides structured data access, Zo provides the reasoning layer. Together they eliminate both the API gap and the logic gap for non-technical builders.

**"Screw this, we'll figure this out later."**
— Alex Forman
*Context:* Candidly describing their initial approach to the variable pricing problem — costs fluctuate wildly per API call based on result volume and anti-bot complexity. Reveals the operational reality driving their imminent shift to usage-based pricing.

## Strategic Questions Raised

**"What if we also wrap that up with a Parse API or a Plaid API — what if it off rip came with the ability to turn any website into an API?"**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Significance:* The core partnership pitch — bundling Parse into the N5OS starter kit for Zo users. Would drive adoption for Parse through Zo's growing non-technical user base while giving Zo users instant web-scraping capability. Both sides benefit from usage-based alignment.

**"To what extent are [users] intrinsically constrained by the backend capabilities of whoever they're using?"**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Significance:* Identifies the key limitation of standalone Parse — without a reasoning/orchestration layer, users hit a ceiling on what they can do with scraped data. Positions Zo as the missing piece that turns Parse from a tool into a system.

**"If you put in headphones and it returns 10,000 products, just the network bandwidth is like 100 times more... it's the same API. How do you price that?"**
— Alex Forman
*Significance:* Surfaces the core pricing challenge Parse faces — identical API calls can have 100x cost variance depending on query specificity and target site behavior. This variable-cost reality is what's pushing them toward usage-based pricing this week.
